title: Conscious possession - the most litigated phrase in NDPS cases
p1:
Most people assume NDPS cases are decided by one simple question: were drugs found or not.
But courts rarely stop there. The real battle often begins after the recovery is shown.
p2:
A common misconception is that mere presence near a substance automatically means possession.
What surprises many is this: the law doesn’t punish proximity, it punishes awareness + control. That’s what ‘conscious possession’ actually tests.
p3:
In simple terms, conscious possession means you knew the substance was there and you had some control over it.
If drugs are found in a shared car, room, or bag, prosecution must still connect knowledge to the accused.
Practical takeaway: being near an object is not enough. Many NDPS cases turn on whether awareness can actually be proven, not just ownership of space.
conclusion: If two people are in a shared space where contraband is found, what should matter more in law: physical proximity or proof of awareness?
